# UC-14.9.11 · Controller Mode Change Detection

## Description

OT security platforms detect when controllers are placed into online, offline, force, or run/stop modes — each a Critical-severity event. A controller taken offline stops controlling its physical process. An unauthorized mode change could halt production, disable safety systems, or prepare for a more destructive attack.

## Value

OT security platforms detect when controllers are placed into online, offline, force, or run/stop modes — each a Critical-severity event. A controller taken offline stops controlling its physical process. An unauthorized mode change could halt production, disable safety systems, or prepare for a more destructive attack.

## Implementation

Both platforms detect controller mode changes across industrial protocols. Alert immediately — CPU Stop and Offline events are highest priority as they halt physical processes. Cross-reference with scheduled maintenance. Track frequency of mode changes per controller. Investigate any mode change from unexpected source IPs.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` · Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert` (type_id="PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING").
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Both platforms detect controller mode changes across industrial protocols. Alert immediately — CPU Stop and Offline events are highest priority as they halt physical processes. Cross-reference with scheduled maintenance. Track frequency of mode changes per controller. Investigate any mode change from unexpected source IPs.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=nozomi sourcetype="nozomi:alert" type_id IN ("PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-MODE*", "PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-RUN*", "PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-STOP*")
| table _time, name, src_ip, dst_ip, type_id, zone, risk
| sort -_time
```

Understanding this SPL

**Controller Mode Change Detection** — OT security platforms detect when controllers are placed into online, offline, force, or run/stop modes — each a Critical-severity event. A controller taken offline stops controlling its physical process. An unauthorized mode change could halt production, disable safety systems, or prepare for a more destructive attack.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` · Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert` (type_id="PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING"). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: nozomi; **sourcetype**: nozomi:alert. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=nozomi, sourcetype="nozomi:alert". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Pipeline stage (see **Controller Mode Change Detection**): table _time, name, src_ip, dst_ip, type_id, zone, risk
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Run the search for a time range that should include both routine and change activity, then compare the same assets, flows, and alerts in the Cisco Cyber Vision UI and in Nozomi Guardian or CMC, matching whichever source the event came from. Reconcile severities, counts, and key fields in both tools.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Mode change timeline with color-coded severity; controller status dashboard; unauthorized source detection.

## SPL

```spl
index=nozomi sourcetype="nozomi:alert" type_id IN ("PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-MODE*", "PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-RUN*", "PROTOCOL-ENGINEERING-STOP*")
| table _time, name, src_ip, dst_ip, type_id, zone, risk
| sort -_time
```

Mode change timeline with color-coded severity; controller status dashboard; unauthorized source detection.
